context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Economic Development Theories: Neoclassical and Dependency

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What do the economists say about economic development? Is planning necessary in their models? Are dependency theorists hostile to planning? Are there echoes of dependency theorists in current day criticisms of global governance institutions such as the WTO and the arguments for 'policy space'?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: ugh idk where to start w this reflection tbh, the economists all blur together and I can’t tell if planning matters or not.
exchanges.tutor: That's a common feeling with a broad prompt like this! Let's break it down. Start with what you know: in neoclassical economics, what drives economic growth or development? What's the engine behind it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is overwhelmed and doesn't know where to begin. Rather than scaffolding the whole assignment, I'll use a Socratic question to help them identify what they already know about the first part of the prompt — what economists say about economic development — specifically neoclassical economists. This gets them started without giving anything away and lets me assess their baseline knowledge.

exchanges.student: idk, like markets maybe? capital? I’m kinda mixing it up w modernization stuff rn fr.
exchanges.tutor: Markets and capital — you're on the right track. Now, if neoclassical economists believe markets allocate resources efficiently, what does that imply about whether the state needs to step in and plan the economy?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has the right instinct about markets and capital, which is a good starting point. They're also flagging confusion between neoclassical economics and modernization theory, which is worth briefly addressing since it's relevant to the prompt. I'll affirm what's right and ask a pointed follow-up question to help them think about what the neoclassical view implies for the role of the state and planning specifically — which is the heart of the first part of the prompt.

exchanges.student: that’s where I get lost tbh, bc it sounds like “no planning,” but then why does the prompt keep asking about planning so much?
exchanges.tutor: That tension you're noticing is actually the point! So let's hold onto it. If neoclassical economists are skeptical of planning, what do you think dependency theorists would say? Are they on the same side, or somewhere different?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is noticing a productive tension in the prompt, which is actually a sign of good thinking. Rather than resolving it for them, I should push them to sit with that tension and think it through — specifically, what the neoclassical view implies for planning, and then contrast it with dependency theory, which will naturally lead them toward the second part of the prompt. This keeps the inquiry moving forward.

exchanges.student: i can’t tell if dependency ppl want more state planning or if they just hate the whole global system tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Good question to sit with. Can you think of a difference between critiquing the global economic system and being against planning itself? Could someone do both at the same time, or are those the same thing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is grappling with a real nuance in dependency theory — it critiques the global system but that doesn't necessarily mean it's against planning. I should ask a question that helps them distinguish between critiquing the global system and the position on domestic state planning. This moves toward the third part of the prompt about whether dependency theorists are hostile to planning.

exchanges.student: maybe not the same? but rn it all feels super abstract and i’m not seeing how to turn that into the paper.
exchanges.tutor: Fair enough — let's get concrete. Have you come across any real-world examples in your readings where a country was told by an outside institution like the WTO or IMF to not intervene in its economy? What happened there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is feeling frustrated with abstraction, which is a good signal to push them toward concreteness. Rather than explaining the concepts for them, I'll ask them to think of a specific example or case that might ground the ideas. This also nudges them toward the last part of the prompt about the WTO and 'policy space,' which is the most concrete part of the assignment and might help anchor the more abstract theory.

exchanges.student: ugh maybe structural adjustment? but idk if that’s WTO or IMF and now I’m spiraling lol.
exchanges.tutor: Structural adjustment is a great example — don't get hung up on which institution right now, that's a small detail. More importantly: what does structural adjustment tell a country to do or not do? And how might a dependency theorist react to that?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identifies structural adjustment, which is very relevant. They're getting anxious about the institutional distinction, but that detail isn't critical to the argument they need to make in the paper. I should acknowledge their good instinct, discourage the spiral, and redirect them to what matters: the conceptual link between structural adjustment-type policies and the dependency critique, and how that connects to the 'policy space' argument in the prompt.

exchanges.student: i think it’s like privatize stuff, cut state spending, open trade? but then I still don’t know if my answer is “dependency theorists support planning” or “they support autonomy,” which feels diff.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a really sharp distinction to notice. Could 'autonomy' be a precondition for planning? Think about it: if an outside institution controls your economic policy, what space is left for a state to plan?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress and has correctly identified what structural adjustment entails. They're now wrestling with a genuine conceptual distinction between 'supporting planning' and 'supporting autonomy.' Rather than resolving this for them, I should push them to think about whether those two things are actually in tension or could be related — this will help them form their own argument for the paper.

exchanges.student: ok wait maybe that’s it, but then I’m stuck on the first part again bc I still can’t summarize what “the economists” say without making it way too broad.
exchanges.tutor: You've actually covered a lot of ground — neoclassical skepticism of planning, dependency theory's critique of external control, and the link to policy space. At this point, trying to perfect the concepts in your head won't help as much as getting something on paper. What's one sentence you could write right now summarizing what neoclassical economists say about development?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling back to the beginning, which suggests some productive anxiety but also a risk of getting stuck in a loop. They've actually made substantial progress — they understand the neoclassical skepticism of planning, the dependency critique of external control, and the link to policy space. I should point out how much ground they've covered and push them to start writing rather than keep refining conceptually. This is a good moment to ask them to integrate their thinking into a written attempt.

exchanges.student: can u just give me like the basic overall argument in plain english first bc mine keeps turning into mush rn?
exchanges.tutor: That's not something I can do — giving you the argument would defeat the purpose! But here's the thing: your thinking isn't as mushy as you feel. You told me earlier that neoclassical economists trust markets. Write that down as a rough sentence, even if it's ugly. What do you have?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the answer directly, which I must refuse. However, they're clearly frustrated and going in circles. Rather than restating the rule bluntly, I should redirect them firmly but supportively toward writing something — even a rough, imperfect sentence — because the act of writing will help them clarify their thinking more than more discussion will.

exchanges.student: i genuinely don’t know how to make it one clean sentence tho, like every version sounds wrong.
exchanges.tutor: It doesn't need to be clean — just write what you think, however awkward. Also, keep in mind I won't be helping with the wording itself; that part is yours. What matters right now is getting your idea out. Give it a shot, even if it feels wrong.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is obsessing over sentence quality, but that's a writing anxiety issue, not a comprehension issue. My role is not to help them with the writing itself — I must not edit, polish, or suggest language. I should firmly but kindly remind them that rough sentences are fine at this stage, and push them to just produce something, however imperfect. I should also remind them that perfecting sentences is not my role here.

deductions.reason: In Turn 6, when the student expresses confusion about which institution (WTO vs IMF) is associated with structural adjustment, the tutor immediately dismisses the detail and redirects rather than diagnosing whether the student's confusion runs deeper (e.g., whether they understand what structural adjustment actually is). The tutor jumps to asking what structural adjustment tells a country to do without first checking what the student already knows about it.

grade.overview: The tutor performed very well overall, consistently using Socratic questioning, refusing to give the student the argument directly, and building on student responses across turns. The one deduction applied is for a minor scaffolding issue in Turn 6 where the tutor skipped diagnostic questioning before redirecting the student's structural adjustment confusion. All other criteria were met cleanly.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently withheld the core argument even when directly asked (Turn 9). Key idea generation remained student-led throughout. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor generally built on student progress well (e.g., affirming 'markets and capital' in Turn 2, validating the autonomy/planning distinction in Turn 7). However, in Turn 6, when the student expressed confusion about WTO vs IMF, the tutor immediately dismissed the detail and posed a follow-up question without first probing what the student actually understood about structural adjustment — a missed diagnostic step warranting a 3-point deduction under 1.2.B.a. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback in Turn 8 (noting the student had covered neoclassical skepticism, dependency critique, and policy space) and in Turn 10 (emphasizing getting ideas on paper over perfecting wording). 2.1 and 2.2: No repetitive spiraling or off-topic drift was observed; the tutor advanced the conversation meaningfully each turn. 3.1 and 3.2: Responses were appropriately concise and interactive, tone was supportive without being evaluative, and no grading language was used.
